coredata.dc:title: Enhanced performance of porphyrin sensitized solar cell based on graphene quantum dots decorated photoanodes
coredata.dc:creator: Sehgal, Preeti
Narula, Anudeep Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Porphyrin sensitized solar cells (PSSC) has been successfully fabricated composed of graphene quantum dots (GQD) functionalized ZnO photoanodes, zinc tetrakis (4-carboxy phenyl)porphyrin (TCPPZn) as sensitizer and polypyrrole coated oxidised multiwalled carbon nanotubes (PPy/OMWCNT) as counter electrode. The effect of the concentration of GQD on the structural, morphological, photophysical and photovoltaic properties of GQD@ZnO, and GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites has also been investigated. Studies indicate that TCPPZn adsorbed on the surface on GQD@ZnO. Hot electron injection mechanism and multiple exciton generation from GQD to ZnO were mainly responsible for the photoexcitation response in PSSC. This study indicates that GQD can play role of sensitizer to some extent. The time decay measurements reveals the evidences of FRET mechanism, and synergistic interaction between GQD and TCPPZn. The Jsc, Voc, PCE of the corresponding PSSC devices enhanced initially and then decreased. Among all the devices fabricated, the PSSC with a 40% amount of GQD (GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn 40%) attains the best performance with the Jsc = 10.1 mA/cm2, Voc = 0.48, PCE = 2.45% FF = 0.507 higher than ZnO@TCPPZn device fabricated without GQD. Overall, this design provides a new concept for the development of photoanodes which derive better efficiency for dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) and PSSC at economical low cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MWCNTs
Hot electron injection
Multiple exciton generation
Photoexcitation
Porphyrin sensitized solar cell
Graphene quantum dots

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of porphyrin sensitized solar cell (PSSC)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was financially supported by Department of Science and Technology (DST-SERB) through SERB/F/0771/2016 . The authors are thankful to Jamia Milia Islamia and Jawaharlal Nehru University for XRD, and PL characterizations respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR Spectra of (a) GQD@ZnO, (b) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(10%), (c) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(20%), (d) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(30%), (e) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(40%), (f) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (50%) composites.
XRD spectra of (a) GQD@ZnO (b) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (10%) (c) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (20%) (d) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (30%) (e) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (40%) (f) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (50%).
X-ray Photo-electron Spectra of (a) GQD and GQD@ZnO nanocomposite (b) TCPPZn (c) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composite (30%) (d) PPy/OMWCNT composite.
Absorption spectra of (a) TCPPZn, GQD, GQD@ZnO nanocomposites (b) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites with different concentrations of GQD (a) 10%, (b) 20%, (c) 30%, (d) 40%, (e) 50% respectively.
Tauc plot of (a) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (10%), (b) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (20%), (c) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (30%), (d) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(40%), (e) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (50%) composites.
(a) Absorption spectrum of fixed concentration of the 1 μM TCPPZn with successive addition of GQDs (b) Absorption spectrum of pure GQD, pure TCPPZn and GQD/TCPPZn with equal concentration.

Photoluminescence spectra of (inset: GQD@ZnO) (a) GQD and GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composites prepared with different concentration of GQD (b) (10%) GQD, (c) (20%)GQD, (d) (30%) GQD, (e) (40%) GQD, (f) (50%) GQD excited at 300 nm.

(a) Absorption spectrum of TCPPZn and emission spectrum of GQD 9Excitation wavelength (380 nm) (b) Emission spectrum of fixed concentration of GQD with successive addition of TCPPZn (excitation at 380 nm) (c) PL Life time of GQD, TCPPZN and GQD/TCPPZn (excitation at 380 nm) (d) PL Life time of ZnO/GQD, ZnO/TCPPZN and ZnO/GQD/TCPPZn (excitation at 380 nm).
Energy level diagram and schematic presentation of energy transfer mechanism in synthesized porphyrin sensitized solar cell.
TEM micrographs of (a) GQDs (b) OMWCNT (c) PPy/OMWCNT composite (d) TCPPZn.
TEM images of (a) GQD@ZnO (b–f) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites with 10%, 20%, 30%, 40% and 50% GQDs respectively.

(A) CV curves of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites (a–e) with various concentrations of GQDs (10%, 20%, 30% 40%, 50%) respectively, GQD@ZnO (f), bare ZnO (g) at a scan rate of 20 mVS−1 in electrolyte containing 0.05 mM LiI, 0.03 M I2, 0.6 M 1-Butyl-3-methyl imidazolium iodide in acetonitrile solution. (B) J-V Curves of fabricated PSSC (a) ZnO/TCPPZn, (b) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (10%), (c) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(20%), (d) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn(30%), (e) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (40%), (f) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (50%) composite under AM1.5 G light illumination of 100 mWCm−2.
Evolution of the characteristics of nine different solar cells fabricated from nine different batches prepared under same conditions.

Nyquist plots of the GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites acetonitrile solution containing electrolyte 0.05 mM LiI, 0.03 M I2, 0.6 M 1-Butyl-3-methyl imidazolium iodide. (A) ZnO, GQD@ZnO under dark and illumination (B) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites (a–e) of various concentrations of GQDs (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%) under dark conditions (C) GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites (a–e) of various concentrations of GQDs (10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, 50%) under AM1.5 G light illumination of 100 mWcm−2.
Optical band gap values of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composites.
J-V Characteristics of GQD@ZnO@TCPPZn composites at different ratios of GQD.

head.ce:title: Enhanced performance of porphyrin sensitized solar cell based on graphene quantum dots decorated photoanodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre of Excellence in Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guru Gobind Singh Indraprastha University, Delhi, 110078, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Excellence in Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Porphyrin sensitized solar cells (PSSC) has been successfully fabricated composed of graphene quantum dots (GQD) functionalized ZnO photoanodes, zinc tetrakis (4-carboxy phenyl)porphyrin (TCPPZn) as sensitizer and polypyrrole coated oxidised multiwalled carbon nanotubes (PPy/OMWCNT) as counter electrode. The effect of the concentration of GQD on the structural, morphological, photophysical and photovoltaic properties of GQD@ZnO, and GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites has also been investigated. Studies indicate that TCPPZn adsorbed on the surface on GQD@ZnO. Hot electron injection mechanism and multiple exciton generation from GQD to ZnO were mainly responsible for the photoexcitation response in PSSC. This study indicates that GQD can play role of sensitizer to some extent. The time decay measurements reveals the evidences of FRET mechanism, and synergistic interaction between GQD and TCPPZn. The Jsc, Voc, PCE of the corresponding PSSC devices enhanced initially and then decreased. Among all the devices fabricated, the PSSC with a 40% amount of GQD (GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn 40%) attains the best performance with the Jsc = 10.1 mA/cm2, Voc = 0.48, PCE = 2.45% FF = 0.507 higher than ZnO@TCPPZn device fabricated without GQD. Overall, this design provides a new concept for the development of photoanodes which derive better efficiency for dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) and PSSC at economical low cost.

ce:section.ce:para: The Dye sensitized solar cells (DSSC) have garnered much attention as an attractive, light weight, low cost solar energy conversion technology [1]. DSSC solar cell design involves photo-electrode comprises of mesoporous wide band gap semiconductor oxide (TiO2, ZnO) inner connected with the dye are packed on a coated transparent conducting glass such as ITO/FTO, in contact with electrolyte usually a redox couple and a counter electrode [2]. Dye sensitizers play an important role as it injects electrons into the conduction band of TiO2, ZnO etc. Porphyrin sensitizers are capable to cover wide spectral range are considered to be cheaper and environmental friendly potentially replacing the expensive ruthenium based metal complex sensitizer [3,4]. Porphyrin has four meso and eight β positions for functionalizations and thus their optical and electrochemical properties can be easily tuned. Photo-electrode is the core of photon electron conversion process, hence play a vital role in determining the overall performance of the cell. A device with appropriate HOMO-LUMO levels, wide absorption profiles influence the light harvesting, electron injection and electron collection efficiencies of DSSC and hence affects the overall performance of the device [5]. Therefore, it is crucial to modify the photoanode by advancing the light harvesting properties with some scatters so that the enhanced efficiency of DSSC can be achieved [6]. ZnO has been widely used as photoanode and is good alternative to TiO2 because of its similar energy band structure and photophysical properties as TiO2, with better electron mobility (205–300 cm2 V/s), higher electron diffusion coefficient (104–106 cm2/s) [7–9]. The large exciton binding energy of ZnO (approx 60 meV) allows efficient excitonic emissions even at room temperature, non toxicity and feasible synthesis of 1D nanostructures makes it a promisable candidate for low threshold and high efficiency photonic devices [10–12]. Modifications with carbon structures such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon nano fibres, carbon quantum dots, graphitic carbon nitride gives significant results. Recently, graphene based materials has also been explored in DSSC for catalytic counter electrodes, non transparent anodes and sensitizers [13–15]. The high visibility, young modulus of graphene, ultrafast electron mobility, tunable band gap and photon absorption strengthens every aspects of photoanode [16]. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms in a two dimensional honeycomb lattice has given enormous attention owing to its high carrier transport mobility, superior mechanical flexibility and good thermal stability. Incorporation of graphene into semiconductor oxide can enhance electron transport while reducing charge recombination and thus increases the DSSC performance [17]. However, their direct application in nanodevices is restricted because graphene sheet do not have an energy band gap which can be explored by converting 2D graphene sheet into zero dimensional quantum dots [18]. GQDs are graphene sheets with size smaller than 100 nm have high surface area grafting by using π-π conjugated network [19]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with quantum confinement, high mobility, tunable band gap, high loading of oxygen atoms, superior photoluminescence (PL) and edge effects have drawn great attention which can be utilized in bio imaging, LEDs, electrocatalysis, and light absorbers in photovoltaics, biosensing and bioimaging [20–22]. Likewise, other carbonaceous structure, carbon nanotubes have been widely used as fillers with conducting polymers due to their high electrical conductivity, chemical stability, low mass density and large surface area. Conducting polymer/carbon nanotubes composites possess superior electronic and optical properties as compare to individual components. Consequently, multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT), a kind of novel nanostructure from of carbon materials with large specific surface area, highly porous and hollow structures, have attracted intense interest as an ideal matrix for depositing PPy [23–25]. PPy/MWCNTs has many benefits over Pt, MWCNTs provide ballistic conductance, strength, stability and also enhances the charge transfer kinetics.

To harness the excellent properties of carbon materials, we have introduced carbon materials in photoelectrodes in the form of GQD and MWCNT. Zinc porphyrin has been used as sensitizer due to its higher short circuit current (JSC) and its first oxidation potential usually lies around +0.8–1.2 V, the upper lying LUMO level of Zn(II) porphyrin lies in better overlap with the ZnO conduction band and increases the driving force for electron injection of the excited zinc porphyrin in the semiconductor. In present study, porphyrin sensitized solar cell (PSSC) has been fabricated which comprises GQD sensitized ZnO as photoanodes and PPY: MWCNT as counter electrode. All the components had been individually reported by many researchers in previous literature viz. Zamiri et al. investigated the role of GQD as photosensitizer as an alternative to N719 dye and achieved an efficiency of 1.077% [26]. Chen et al. investigated the GQD doped polypyrrole counter electrode for DSSC and found that GQDs doped PPy has porous structure and displays higher catalytic current density and lower charge resistance towards electrolyte and achieved the PCE of 5.27% for device PPy doped with 10% GQDs [27]. Foroushani et al. studied the role of ZnTCPP as a sensitizer in combination with ruthenium complexes in co sensitized solar cell and the efficiency of the device after co sensitization was improved to 6.35% [28]. GQDs sensitized PSSC with GQD decorated ZnO photoanode and PPy/MWCNTs as counter electrode and metalloporphyrin replaces dye in a single device is absolute novel concept having a synergistic effect of all the components. To understand the mechanism of GQD and the effect of its concentration on the optical and photovoltaic properties, different photoanodes with different concentrations of GQD were prepared.

Solvents used in the synthesis and purification were supplied by Merck and freshly distilled prior to use. The multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT, 98%, OD: 40–50 nm, length: 0.5–500 Im), pyrrole monomer (Py, 98%) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Anhydrous ferric chloride (FeCl3, 97%), sodium hydroxide and citric acid were purchased from Fischer scientific. Tetrakis (4-carboxy phenyl)porphyrin (TCPP, 97%) was purchased from TCI Chemicals was used as received.

GQD was synthesized by direct pyrolyzing of Citric acid as reported in literature [29]. Citric acid (2.0 gm) was melted at 200–210 °C using silicone oil bath. After 5 min, its color changed from colorless to pale yellow and after 30 min color changed to orange color indicating the formation of GQD. Drop wise melt of citric acid was poured into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution with constant stirring. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.0 with NaOH solution. The light yellow color solution with blue emission under UV light was obtained.

GQD@ZnO composite were prepared by insitu method. To achieve the optimum efficiency and evaluate the effect of composition of GQD on photoanode, different percentage (v/v) of GQD as visible light sensitizer was added to prepare GQD@ZnO composite. Zinc acetate dihydrate [Zn (CH3COO)2]·2H2O was dissolved in isopropanol at 60 °C. After complete dissolution of zinc acetate dihydrate, different amount of GQD solution was added and thereafter NaOH also dissolved in isopropanol was added to the reaction mixture with constant stirring. After continuous stirring for another 2 h, the reaction mixture became turbid and pale yellow-whitish color solution was obtained. Stirring was continued for another 3 h, after that precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with distilled water and isopropanol for several times to dissolve other impurities. The precipitate was dried under vaccum for 6 h.

The metallation process involves deformation of the TCPP ring, exchange of solvent molecule with first pyrrolenine nitrogen atom, chelate ring closure followed by deprotonation of nitrogen atoms which leads to the formation of TCPPZn. A mixture of porphyrin (TCPP) (79.07 mg, 0.01 mmol) in chloroform and zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn (ac) 2.2H2O) (39.5 mg, 0.18 mmol) in methanol (10 mL) was stirred at room temperature for 4 h. After removal of solvent under reduced pressure, purple color precipitates were obtained.

GQD@ZnO@TCPPZn composites were prepared by insitu method. Zinc acetate dihydrate [Zn (CH3COO)2]·2H2O was dissolved in methanol. After complete dissolution of zinc acetate dihydrate, TCPPZn dissolved in chloroform was added to the reaction mixture and reaction was continued to stir for another 3 h. Precipitates was centrifuged washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times to dissolve the impurities. The precipitate was dried under vaccum for 4 h.

One dimensional hybrid PPY/OMWCNT nanocomposite was prepared by insitu chemical oxidative polymerization. This composite possess a core/shell structure in which, OMWCNT possess core and PPy acts as shell. To prepare PPy/OMWCNT composite, multiwalled carbon nanotubes were first purified by heating at 400 °C in air to remove any carbonaceous impurities and then refluxed for 16 h at 90 °C with 8 N HNO3 to improve the adhesion between the polypyrrole and carbon nanotubes by π-π interaction. To get the neutral pH, product was washed with deionised water and final product was dried in vaccum. To prepare PPY/OMWCNT composites, OMWCNT was first dispersed in 20 mL deionised water and ultrasonication was done for 30 min. Subsequently, pyrrole monomer was added to this dispersion followed by slow addition of FeCl3 which act as an oxidant. The reaction mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature. The black residue was filtered, washed with distilled water and acetone till all the oligomers were washed away. The mass ratio of PPy: OMWCNT were taken 1:1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of porphyrin sensitized solar cell (PSSC)
ce:section.ce:para:
The device was fabricated with structure of ITO/GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn/LiI/PPy@OMWCNT using solution process. ITO substrates were cleaned in detergent, de-ionized water, acetone and isopropanol under ultrasonication for 15 min. The synthesized GQD@ZnO nanocomposite was dissolved in IPA and the prepared paste was spin coated on ITO substrates at 2000 rpm for 60 s. After air drying for 25 min, the sample was kept in oven for sintering (250 °C for 30 min). After cooling to room temperature, the GQD sensitized films were immersed in TCPPZn solution at room temperature for 12 h. The films were washed with isopropanol to remove excess dye on the surface and vaccum dried to get sensitized photoanode. Thus dye sensitized GQD@ZnO photoanodes were obtained. PSSC was assembled with counter electrode composed of PPy/OMWCNT composites. PPy/OMWCNT composite was mixed with 50 mL of isopropanol under ultrasonication for 15 min and then spin coated on ITO substrate at 2000 rpm and air dried for 30 min. The electrolyte containing 0.05 mM LiI, 0.03 M I2, 0.6 M 1-Butyl-3-methyl imidazolium iodide was injected into the space between the sensitized GQD@ZnO photoanode and PPY/CNT counter electrode which were clamped together with a clip to form a sandwich type cell. Parafilm was used as spacer between the photoanode and counter electrode. The active area of cell was 0.25 cm2. The efficiency, fill factor were calculated by:
(1) FF = Jmax Vmax/Jsc Voc
(2) η(%) = Jsc Voc FF/Pin * 100
whereas Jsc is photocurrent density at short circuit voltage, Voc is open circuit voltage, Jmax is the current density, Vmax is the voltage associated with the maximum power, Jsc is the short current density.

Fourier transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) characterization was carried out to examine the structural determination of composites using Shimadzu IR affinity 1 S Spectrophotometer in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 on KBr pellet. X-ray diffraction powder (XRD) measurement of composites were carried out with a PAN Analytical Diffractometer model Imperial using CuKα radiation with a scanning speed of 10°/min and recorded in the 2ϴ range of 10°–100°. Ultra-Violet Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis) spectra of nanocomposites were recorded using a Hitachi U-2900 Spectrophotometer. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded from Varian Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer. Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss EVO 18) was used to study the morphology of the nanocomposites. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was carried out using a JEOL (JEM-200 CX) (LEO-440) using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The samples for TEM were prepared by dispersing the product in isopropyl alcohol. X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) with a radiation source of Al Kα (1486.7 eV) was used to determine the surface chemical compositions. The electrochemical measurements were performed on electrochemical workstation Corrtest (CS 2350) potentiostat using three electrode cells, where GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn coated ITO substrates were used as working electrodes, Pt wire and Ag/AgCl were used as counter electrode and reference electrode respectively. Working electrodes were prepared by drop casting the nanocomposite solution on ITO substrates having 1 × 1 cm2. A liquid electrolyte containing 0.05 mM LiI, 0.03 M I2, 0.6 M 1-Butyl-3-methyl imidazolium iodide in acetonitrile was used as electrolyte. Impedance spectra were recorded by applying an AC voltage of 50 mV in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–106 Hz. EIS measurements were recorded in dark and under illumination using Oriel (96110) 300 W solar stimulator. The light intensity was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2. Cyclic voltammetric (CV) measurements were conducted at a scan rate of 20 mVS−1 in the voltage range of −2 V to +2 V Vs Ag/AgCl. The PSSC Current density-voltage (J-V) characteristic was measured using Keithley 2450 source measurement unit under standard AM 1.5 light illumination using Oriel (91160) 300 W solar stimulator as the excitation source. The light intensity was calibrated to 100 mW/cm2.

Fig. 1 shows the Fourier transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of GQD, GQD@ZnO, and GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposite. In case of GQD (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information) the broad band at 3400 cm−1 and 1078 cm−1 indicates the presence of large amount of hydroxyl group and C-OH groups respectively. The absorption band at 1588 cm−1 is attributed to C=C skeletal vibration of graphene. The C-OH stretching vibration was observed at 1292 cm−1 and bending vibration at 1400 cm−1 is corresponding to CH2. The stretching vibration of aryl ether (C-O-C) was observed at 1078 cm−1 which gets shifted to 1062 cm−1 incase of GQD@ZnO nanocomposite. FTIR spectra of GQD@ZnO confirmed the strong interaction between GQD and ZnO. Broad band at 3600 cm−1 represents hydroxyl group. The symmetrical vibrations of COO− at 1385 cm−1 suggesting that acetic ions are adsorbed on ZnO surface. Furthermore the peak at 470 cm−1 depicted the Zn-O stretching vibration. Incase, of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites, all the peaks of GQD@ZnO and zinc metalloporphyrin are observed with slightly shifted positions confirmed the chemical interaction between the components. The broad band at 3439 cm−1 is associated to O-H stretching vibration of GQD. The C=N and C=C stretching vibrations of porphyrin give rise to five absorption peaks in the region from 1600 to 1400 cm−1. However, the number of these absorption peaks reduced in GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposite. The peaks at 1045 cm−1, and 941 cm−1 is attributed to inplane porphyrin deformation which slightly shifted from 1072,997 cm−1. Out of plane deformation band of zinc porphyrin (TCPPZn) also shifted from 805 cm−1 to 831 cm−1 (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information). The peaks at 1388 cm−1, 470 cm−1,1556,1506 is attributed to the stretching vibration of Zn-O and amide band-I and amide band-II respectively. The presence of these peaks in nanocomposite confirmed strong chemical interaction between GQD@ZnO with the TCPPZn in GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composite. However, upon increasing the concentration of GQDs in the composite, no significant changes in the FTIR spectrum of the composite was observed. The FTIR spectra of polypyrrole and O-MWCNT (Fig. S2 in Supplementary Information) shows the characteristic band at 3400 cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching. The peaks at 1691, 1286 and 1140 cm−1 were consistent with C=O stretching and C-C-O stretching respectively. The characteristic peaks of C-N, C-C asymmetric and symmetric ring stretching were observed at 1535 and 1456 cm−1 and C-H inplane bending vibration and C-H ring deformation of polypyrrole in PPY/OMWCNT nanocomposite. The peak at 1691 cm−1 is due to the C=O functional group introduced by acid group treatment of MWCNT. The carboxylic group provides interfacial interaction due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between –COOH groups of OMWCNTS and NH group of PPy in MWCNTs-PPy composites [30].

Fig. 2 depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) of GQD@ZnO and GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposite. The X-ray pattern for TCPPZn displayed sharp peaks at 12.62°, 14.88°, 17.80°, 19.56°, 21.13°, 22.86°, 26.75°, 30.29°, 43.4° is corresponding to 0.7 nm, 0.59 nm, 0.49 nm, 0.45 nm, 0.42 nm, 0.38 nm, 0.33 nm, 0.29 nm, 0.2 nm respectively indicated that porphyrin was polycrystalline and monophasic in nature (Fig. S3 in supporting information). The diffraction peaks of both ZnO and GQD can be observed in GQD@ZnO composite confirmed the formation of GQD@ZnO composites. The peak at 32.12° corresponds to (002) plane confirmed the formation of hexagonal wurtzite structure of ZnO. However, the relative intensity of GQD is corresponding to (002) plane was very low in GQD@ZnO which further confirmed the formation of GQD@ZnO. Incase of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composites, the diffraction pattern comprised the peaks of GQD@ZnO, moreover, the intensity of peaks was increased with the adsorption of TCPPZn onto GQD@ZnO. The diffraction pattern showed Bragg's peaks at 31.79, 34.49, 36.32, and 47.60,56.55, 62.99, 66.34 is attributed to 0.28 nm, 0.25 nm, 0.24 nm, 0.19 nm 0.16, 0.14 nm, 0.14 nm reflections respectively. These results confirmed that TCPPZn adsorb on the surface of GQD@ZnO hardly has any effect on the crystal structure of GQD@ZnO. The x-ray diffraction pattern of oxidised multiwalled carbon nano tubes displayed the presence of two peaks at 2ϴ = 25.82° (3.413 Å) and 43.40° (2.083 Å) is corresponding to (002) and (100) diffractions which can be attributed to interlayer spacing of the nanotube and reflections of the carbon atoms respectively (Fig. S4 in supporting Information). In PPY/OMWCNT composites, the intensity of peaks was decreased and the diffraction pattern showed amorphous nature of composites due to polypyrrole. The diffractogram depicted a relatively broad peak at 25.73° and a very small peak at 42.72° (Fig. S4 in supporting information) confirmed the presence of PPy and OMWCNT in the composite.

X-ray photoelectron Spectra (XPS) was further used to determine the chemical states and bonding configuration of the composites. The survey spectrum of GQD showed the presence of C1S and O1S as most prominent peaks (Fig. 3 (a)). C1S spectrum of GQD could be deconvoluted into three individual peaks arising from sp2 and sp3 domains assigning to C-C (284.5 eV), C=O group (287.5eV), and COOH group (289.2 eV) indicates that the prepared GQD are rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxyl groups. The O1S spectrum at 531.5 eV and a small peak at 536 eV can be assigned to chemisorbed oxygen of surface hydroxyl groups. The presence of oxygen rich groups make the GQD soluble in aqueous medium and further functionalizations can also be done [31]. Incase of GQD@ZnO (Fig. 3(a)), the shift in C1S to higher binding energy level at 288 eV and 291.5 eV is attributed to decrease in electron density on C atom due to electrostatic interaction GQD-ZnO interface. The O1S peaks also shifts to higher binding energy attributed to the electrostatic interaction. In addition to C1S and O1S peaks, new peaks appeared at 1047 eV and 1023 eV corresponding to Zn2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 respectively is attributed to the formation of Zn-O-C bond. These peaks confirmed the chemical interaction of GQD with ZnO nanorods. Incase of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites (Fig. 3(c)), all the peaks indexed to C1S, O1S, Zn 2p, Zn 2p3/2, and Zn 3d was shifted to lower binding energy as compare to GQD@ZnO and TCPPZn alone, which is attributed to the covalent interactions between GQD@ZnO and TCPPZn. In TCPPZn (Fig. 3(b)), the C1S peak was observed at 284.5eV, incase of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn C1S peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.5eV, 285.5eV, 288.5eV. The N1S peak in TCPPZn is observed at 400eV while incase of composites, the N1S intensity was very low and appeared at 398.8eV. Incase of TCPPZn Zn 3d peak was obtained at 10.4eV while incase of composite sharp peak of Zn3d was observed at 10eV. Likewise, the lower binding energies can also be observed for Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 as compare to TCPPZn. The lower Zn2+ binding energy values in composites can be attributed to change in bond character of Zn. The appearance of Zn 3d peaks and change in binding energy values confirmed the formation of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites. From the high resolution N 1 S core level spectrum of PPy/OMWCNT (Fig. S4 in Supporting information), the peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies about 399.52 and 402.06 eV is attributed to the benzenoid nitrogen (-N-H-) in the pyrrole ring and positively charged nitrogen (N+H−) and (=N+ H) species respectively. This result indicates that polypyrrole was successfully coated on OMWCNT.

To gain the basic understanding of photophysical properties of nanocomposites, absorption and photoluminescence spectra were acquired at room temperature. Fig. 4 (A) shows the absorption spectra of GQD, GQD@ZnO, and TCPPZn. Absorption spectrum of GQD displays two absorption peaks at 230 nm and 353 nm with a band gap of 2.87 eV is attributed to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domain and n-π* transitions of C = O respectively. The ZnO exhibits a strong absorption in ultra-violet (UV) region at 308 nm with a bang gap of 3.37eV. After loading GQDs in GQD@ZnO hybrid, the absorption peak was shifted to higher wavelength with band gap of 3.31eV. The reduced band gap in GQD@ZnO composites can be accredited to additional energy states induced below the conduction band level and above the valence band of ZnO [32]. The GQD@ZnO composites showed two more pronounced absorption peaks at 245 nm and 300 nm as compare to GQD and ZnO alone, confirming the interaction of ZnO and GQD. The absorption spectrum of TCPPZn shows one soret band at 425 nm and two Q bands at 558 nm and 598 nm confirmed the metallation of Zn to porphyrin ring as porphyrin ring deprotonates forms dianionic ligand on metallation. The metal ions behave as lewis acid accepts lone pair of electrons forms dianionic porphyrin ligand [33]. GQD has carboxyl and OH groups which acts as a linker to bind GQD on the ZnO surface results in less adsorption sites left for TCPPZn attachment onto ZnO surface and thus light absorption intensity and dye loading capacity in composites is lower in composites. This suggests that an optimal amount of GQD is required to explore the optical properties of composites. Incase of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites Fig. 4(B), absorption peaks of porphyrin at 425 nm and 556 nm corresponded to Soret band and Q band respectively were prominent only in 10% and 20% GQD. As can be seen, the intensity of soret band was decreased and the Q band was not appeared in 30%, 40%, and 50% GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composites. Furthermore, the absorption peaks at 349–357 nm and 230–270 nm is attributed to π-π* and n-π* transitions respectively were slightly shifted to higher wavelength with lower band gap values indicates the additional energy levels between the GQD@ZnO and TCPPZn. The optical band gap values were determined by using Tauc relation summarised in Table 1 and represented by Fig. 5 .
(3) αhν = B (hν-Egap)n
where α is absorption coefficient, hν is photon energy, Egap is optical band gap and n = 2 or ½ for indirect and direct allowed transitions respectively. Band gap values were determined by method described by Ghobadi [34]. After substitutions we can write:
(4) (Abs/λ) 1/n = B(1/λ – 1/λgap)
where λ is wavelength, Abs. is corresponding value of measured absorbance. λgap can be calculated from curve (Abs/λ)1/n versus 1/λ at condition (Abs/λ)1/m = 0. The band gap value was obtained using equation:
(5) Eg = 1239.83/λg

To further examine the light absorption behaviour, absorption spectrums of fixed concentration of TCPPZn as a function of increasing concentration of GQDs are recorded in Fig. 6 (a). In Fig. 6(a) the absorption intensity of TCPPZn increases upon successive addition of GQDs which indicates that GQDs serves as an additional light absorber and light absorption can be enhanced by the synergism of GQD and TCPPZn at optimal concentration. The enhancement in absorption is due to from chemical coupling between these two materials as shown in Fig. 6(b). In FRET mechanism, the absorbed energy by two components redistributes themselves and the absorption intensity of mixed solution should be equal to the absorption intensity of individual components [35].

Fig. 7 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of GQD and composites. The PL spectra of GQD emerges from zig zag sites with triplet ground state and quantum confinement effect, Many factors such as the size of GQD, synthesis method, pH, defects due to –OH, epoxy, ether, C=O, and –CO groups affects the photoluminescence property of GQD [36]. The synthesized GQD show bright blue fluorescence under UV light at 350 nm. The GQD exhibits pronounced excitation at 350 nm and relatively broader emission peak at 425 nm is attributed to the π-π stacking interactions. The excitation dependent PL behaviour is common characteristic of fluorescent carbon materials [37]. Different size, shape, and defects exhibit different PL emission behaviour. The up-conversion of GQD is one of the characteristic behaviour of GQD. The up conversion emission peak of GQD starts from 720 nm excitation is contributed to multi photon active process (Fig. S6 in Supporting Information) [38,39]. When these GQD combine with ZnO in GQD@ZnO composites, the emission from GQD was completely quenched in visible region. In addition, a sharp distinct peak at 347 nm with two shoulders at 406 nm and 431 nm was observed which confirmed the presence of GQD in GQD@ZnO nanocomposites (inset Fig. 7). The peak at 347 nm is corresponded to the emission peak of ZnO which is a blue shift from generally reported emission peak of ZnO (379 nm) in previous literature [40]. The PL quenching for GQD@ZnO composite is attributed to the blocking effect by the GQD on ZnO surface is corresponding to the interfacial charge separation at GQD/ZnO interface [41]. PL quenching and appearance of shoulder peaks at 406 nm and 431 nm elucidates the functionalization of GQD onto ZnO surface. PL spectrum of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites shows a strong and distinct peak at 350 nm with small shoulders at 421 nm, 570 nm and 640 nm.The shoulder peaks at 570 nm and 640 nm can be attributed to zinc porphyrin peaks. The presence of these peaks at 570 nm and 640 confirmed the formation of composites. When TCPPZn was functionalized with GQD@ZnO, the interactions between GQD and TCPPZn occurred with change in their chemical environment and as a result the symmetry of porphyrin gets changed results in shifting of wavelength and also confirmed the adsorption of porphyrin onto GQD@ZnO surface. The bathochromic shift was observed for PL transitions at 350 nm with increase in GQD concentration is corresponded to increase in intermolecular interactions between GQD and ZnO.

Furthermore, GQDs can serve as energy transfer antennas to accept and radiate energy to TCPPZn. To achieve the pre requisite condition for FRET, there is good overlap between the absorption spectrum of TCPPZn and emission spectrum of GQDs (Fig. 8 (a)). Since charge transfer depends upon the density of ZnO and GQDs this leads to PL quenching in UV region and stronger PL at 380 nm. A Fluorescence spectrum of fixed concentration of GQD with increasing concentration of TCPPZn was recorded to analyze the energy transfer between the GQD and TCPPZn. The PL intensity of GQDs solution quenches with successive addition of TCPPZn which confirmed the resonance energy transfer from GQD to TCPPZn (Fig. 8(b)). The average life time of GQD is 2.62 ns whereas the average lifetime of TCPPZn is 1.5 ns and for GQD-TCPPZn solution the average life time is 2.24 ns (Fig. 8(c)). The average life time of GQD is shortened and it prolonged for TCPPZn in TCPP/GQD solution suggesting the non radiative energy transfer from GQD to TCPPZn. The FRET mechanism was further explored in presence of ZnO. The time resolved PL measurements at excitation wavelength 380 nm showed the average life time of TCPPZn-ZnO is 1.3 ns which is faster than compared to the hybrid electrode GQD@ZnO@TCPPZn where average life time is 2.19 ns (Fig. 8(d)). In this mechanism, there is a possibility that GQDs transfer energy to TCPPZn through radiative energy transfer (RET) as there is no direct FRET possible between ZnO and GQD because the band gap of ZnO is higher (3.2eV) than the GQD (2.5eV) and the energy from GQD cannot excite the ground state electrons of ZnO. This picture suggests that the energy transfer from GQD to TCPPZn can be either by FRET or by RET. The relative energy efficiency between GQD and TCPPZn has been calculated to 72.4% by FRET and 11.3% by RET. Therefore the energy transfers mainly due to the FRET mechanism. The schematic of energy transfer and electron transport process in PSSC has been presented in Fig. 9 .

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed to investigate the morphological changes after GQD and TCPPZn sensitization on ZnO nanorods. ZnO exhibits smooth and denser nanorods [Inset Fig. 11(e)] while TCPPZn is spherical in shape [Fig. 10 (d)]. Furthermore, the TEM image of PPY/OMWCNT composite clearly shows that OMWCNT core coated with PPy shell Fig. 10(c). The TEM image of GQD shows that leaf like template is composed of several thin branched neuron structure (Fig. 10(a)). GQD@ZnO composite clearly shows the network of thin tubular ZnO nanorods with black spots entangled into tubular network. The dark spots in Fig. 11 (a) represents the TEM image of GQD@ZnO where GQD was randomly dispersed onto ZnO surface. The TEM image of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn reveals the strong interactions of all the components [Fig. 11(b–f)]. The dark domain reveals the presence of GQD while the bright domain represents the TCPPZn in composites. After sensitization of GQD@ZnO with TCPPZn, morphological changes can be clearly seen with the transformation of reticulate to porous structure.

Fig. 12 (a) shows the CV curves of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn with a Pt counter electrode at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1 in electrolyte containing 0.05 mM LiI, 0.03 M I2, 0.6 M 1-Butyl-3-methyl imidazolium iodide (BMII) in acetonitrile solution. Knowing the compatibility of porphyrin sensitizers with cobalt electrolytes, iodide redox couple has been choosen as I−/I3 − showed better efficiencies with less sensitization time as compare to other redox copules in some literature besides it involves green chemistry [41]. To improve the photovoltaic efficiency, BMII was used as electrolyte additive as it enhances the physical diffusion of I− and I3 − ions. Previous literature shows that BMII as additive can increase the efficiency by 18.4% [42]. Photo-generated electrons from dyes are injected into GQD decorated ZnO conduction band and the oxidised dyes are reduced by I− ion to I3 − ions in the electrolyte. These I3 − ions on the counter electrode are reduced. Additionally, another mechanism can also be possible as suggested by FRET mechanism. It can be observed from Fig. 12(a) that the peak currents of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn are higher indicating that GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn has higher electrocatalytic activity and more reaction active centres for reduction current of I3 − ions than GQD@ZnO and bare ZnO. It is also known that larger enclosed redox reaction area represents larger electrochemically active surface area of an electrode [43]. Furthermore, the redox current was increased with increase in GQD content until (40% GQD) and then afterwards decreased. The initial redox peak current can be attributed to the GQDs while after more GQD incorporation leads to the aggregation and reduce the active sites towards the I/I3 − redox reactions. These results are also consistent with the photovoltaic measurements.

Fig. 12(b) displays the photocurrent density-voltage (J-V) curves of the PSSC with different GQD concentration. The device parameters extracted from Fig. 12(b) are tabulated in Table 2 . To evaluate the role of GQD, ZnO@TCPPZn device was also fabricated and compared with GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn device. The ZnO@TCPPZn revealed only 0.817% PCE with Voc = 0.37, Jsc = 7.2 mA/cm2 with FF = 0.307. On addition of GQD, better efficiency was achieved and the PCE of the PSSC was increased to 2.45%. The efficiency of the device was increased with increase in concentration of GQD but after a certain amount, the efficiency of the device was decreased. The increased efficiency of PSSC after incorporation of GQD can be ascribed to increase in electron transfer rate because sp2 network of GQD provides an effective pathway for electron transportation. It provides longer electron free mean path and less recombination which improves charge collection efficiency [44]. The GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (40%) device shows Jsc = 10.1 mA/cm2, Voc = 0.48, FF = 0.507, η = 2.45%. The increase in photovoltaic parameters with increase in GQD content can be attributed to hot electron injection mechanism. The HOMO level of the GQD lies with 5.1–5.4 eV matches well with the band level of ZnO and reduction potential of I3 −/I− electrolyte [35,45,46]. Therefore, hot electron injection could be possible between GQD and ZnO. Under illumination, GQD could inject electron into the conduction band (CB) of ZnO in a similar manner as TCPPZn and can restore from the electrolyte [47]. Furthermore, GQD can generate multiple excitons from single photon and can inject into the CB of ZnO and thus significantly enhanced the photovoltaic properties. The photovoltaic performance of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (10%) was very close to ZnO@TCPPZn because it has only 10% GQD. In this case TCPPZn played a major role in generating photogenerated electrons. As the concentration of GQD was increased, more GQDs adsorbed on ZnO surface leads to less adsorption sites available for TCPPZn, simultaneously, the hot electron injection mechanism from GQD to ZnO was increased results into improved photovoltaic performance. Table 2 also indicates that FF was gradually increased with increase in GQD content is attributed to the faster electron charge transfer rate provided by the GQD. However, after an optimal concentration of GQDs (GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn 40%), further addition of GQDs leads to aggregation of GQDs and its electron injection characteristics is lost [48]. Incase of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (50%), the electron injected in ZnO CB could be only due to the electron generated by TCPPZn. These results indicates that proper amount of GQD can play significant role of sensitizer to some extent at economical cost while maintaining environmental concerns. It is an initial effort towards demonstrating the potential of environmentally friendly GQD as light harvesting materials for next generation DSSCs. However, the efficiency of the device is low as compare to previous literature viz Salam et al. [49] fabricated TiO2 with GQD using N719 dye and achieved an efficiency of 6.22%, Lee et al. [50] prepared GQDs using selective approach and fabricated DSSC and achieved an efficiency of 7.28%; but the advantage of presented technique is the synergistic effect of GQD and Zinc porphyrin. Zinc porphyrins can be a good alternative to hazardous ruthenium dyes and with further optimizations with GQDs better efficiencies can be achieved. To improve the light harvesting capacity of GQD and optimizing device configuration to achieve improved photovoltaic parameters is under study. The reproducibility of this technique was evaluated by the measurements of Voc, Jsc, FF and η within a series of 10 different cells prepared from 10 different batches. The deviations for all solar cell characteristics are relatively narrow with Voc, and FF is more stable than Jsc (Fig. 13 ).

To further analyze the interface properties of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn nanocomposites, EIS measurements were performed under dark and under illumination. Generally, the Nyquist for electrode-electrolyte interface contain two semicircles and a straight line at lower frequencies. The straight line observes by diffusion process while the semicircles in mid frequency and high frequency range correspond to charge transfer process and resistance by the electrolyte respectively [51]. Previous literature reveals that smaller the radius of the arc in mid frequency region, the faster is the charge transfer occur between the electrode and electrolyte [52]. As can be seen from Nyquist plot, Fig. 14 (A), under dark conditions, the radius of arc for pristine ZnO is highest indicates that ZnO had highest charge transfer resistance. The Rct values for GQD@ZnO were lower than the pure ZnO corresponds to faster charge process in GQD@ZnO. Under illumination, the Rct values of ZnO, GQD@ZnO was lower down implying that there was an increase in charge transfer rate at the interface and effective dissociation of photogenerated exciton takes place at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Moreover, the Rct values for GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (Fig. 14(B)) nanocomposites were further lower attributed to the higher electron transfer ability. Under illumination, the Rct values of the composites were further decreased indicating that upon illumination electron transfer takes place at faster rate and there was less recombination at the interface. The decrease in the radius of arc indicates the reduction in electron recombination and enhancement in electron transport efficiency at the interface. This decrease in radius of Rct was observed till GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (30%) (Fig. 14(C)) after that the Rct values was increased for GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (40% and 50% composites) which can be attributed to increase in trap sites that obstructs the movement of electrons.

Porphyrin sensitized solar cell GQD@ZnO/TCCPPZn was successfully fabricated with different amount of GQD@ZnO photoanode. GQD was functionalized onto ZnO surface to form different GQD containing photoanodes and their photovoltaic studies were evaluated. Absorption and PL studies confirmed the formation of GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn composites. The intensity of soret band decreased with increase in GQD concentration in composites. The PL measurements also confirmed the interactions of GQD@ZnO with TCPPZn. The bathochromic shift in PL transitions at 350 nm was observed with increase in GQD concentration indicates the increase in molecular interactions between GQD and ZnO. The XPS and XRD studies also confirmed the molecular interactions of GQD@ZnO and TCPPZn. Weak molecular interactions exhibits between GQD and TCPPZn. These studies indicates that TCPPZn adsorb on surface of GQD@ZnO. The GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn hybrids display outstanding photovoltaic conversion efficiency which is higher than the PSSC fabricated without GQD. Electron transfer mainly occurred through hot electron injection method. The photovoltaic parameters initially increased and then decreased after an optimal concentration of GQDs in composite. The best photovoltaic efficiency was obtained for composite having 40% GQDs. The GQD@ZnO/TCPPZn (40%) PSSC shows Jsc = 10.1 mA/cm2, Voc = 0.48, FF = 0.507, η = 2.45%. Thus, GQD can acts as sensitizer to some extent and by further optimizing their properties; it can be used in dye sensitized solar cell (DSSC) and PSSC to achieve better efficiencies.
There is no conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by Department of Science and Technology (DST-SERB) through SERB/F/0771/2016. The authors are thankful to Jamia Milia Islamia and Jawaharlal Nehru University for XRD, and PL characterizations respectively.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.04.005.
